subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Partisan Attitudes on Compromise
stimulus: Source: American National Election Studies 2016 Pilot, January 2016. Bar graph showing the percentage of Democrats, Republicans, and Independents who believe elected officials should compromise versus stick to their principles. Key data: approximately 55% of Republicans believe officials should stick to principles compared to approximately 34% of Democrats. Democrats show higher support for compromise than Republicans.

parts.prompt: Identify the political affiliation of people who are most likely to believe elected officials should compromise.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Identifies Democrats as the political affiliation most likely to believe elected officials should compromise.
parts.scoring_notes: Only Democrats earns the point. Republicans or Independents do not earn the point.

parts.prompt: Describe the difference between Democrats and Republicans on their attitudes of whether government officials should stick to their principles, based on the data in the bar graph.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Describes that more Republicans than Democrats think government officials should stick to their principles. Accept responses that cite specific percentages (approximately 55% of Republicans compared to 34% of Democrats) or describe the directional difference.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any accurate description of the difference between Democrats and Republicans on this attitude. The response must use the data, not just describe general partisan differences.

parts.prompt: Explain how the data in the bar graph could influence how a Republican candidate would shift his or her campaign positions after securing the Republican nomination for president.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Explains that because Republican voters prefer officials to stick to principles, a candidate will likely take firm conservative positions in the primary, but after securing the nomination may shift toward more moderate/compromise positions to appeal to the broader general election electorate including Democrats and Independents who prefer compromise.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the specific shift from primary to general election strategy based on the data. Generic statements about candidates changing positions do not earn the point.

parts.prompt: Explain how the data in the bar graph could affect policy making interactions between the president and Congress.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Explains how partisan attitudes toward compromise vs. sticking to principles affect policymaking. Accept: policy making is easier when president and Congress are from the same party (unified government); policy making is harder with divided government; the president or Congress may compromise to appeal to Democrats/Independents who prefer compromise.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must connect the data about partisan attitudes to a specific effect on president-Congress policymaking interactions. Accept any plausible and accurate explanation grounded in the data.
